question: 60 words can be made using all the letters of the word BHBJO, with or without meaning. If these words are written as in a dictionary, then the $50^{\text {th }}$ word is :
solution:
$\mathrm{B} \mathrm{B} \mathrm{H} \mathrm{J} \mathrm{O}$
B____$4 !=24$
H____$\frac{4 !}{2 !}=12$
j____$\frac{4 !}{2 !}=12$
O B B H J
O B B J H $\rightarrow 50^{\text {th }}$ rank

subject: Mathematics
q: In an examination, there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> multiple choice questions with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> choices, out of which exactly one is correct. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> marks for each correct answer, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></math> marks for each wrong answer and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> mark if the question is not attempted. Then, the number of ways a student appearing in the examination gets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> marks is _____
question: In an examination, there are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> multiple choice questions with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> choices, out of which exactly one is correct. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> marks for each correct answer, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></math> marks for each wrong answer and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> mark if the question is not attempted. Then, the number of ways a student appearing in the examination gets <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> marks is _____
solution:
Let the marks obtained in each of the five MCQ be x1, x2, x3, x4, x5

Now, x1+x2+x3+x4+x5=5

Only one possibility satisfies the above equation i.e.  3,3,3,-2,-2

Hence, total number of ways =5!3!2!×2×2=40

subject: Mathematics
q: The total number of three-digit numbers, divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, which can be formed using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>8</mn></math>, if repetition of digits is allowed, is
question: The total number of three-digit numbers, divisible by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, which can be formed using the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>8</mn></math>, if repetition of digits is allowed, is
solution:
Given,

Three-digit number to be formed which are divisible by 3 using the digits 1,3,5,8 and repetition is allowed,

Now taking case 1, where all digits are same, we get

1,1,1, 3,3,3, 5,5,5, 8,8,8→4 ways

Now taking case 2, where 2-digit are same and one is distinct, we get

5,5,8→3!2!=3 ways, 8,8,5→ 3 ways

Now taking case 3, where all are distinct, we get

1,3,5→6 ways, 1,8,3→6 ways

So, total ways will be 4+3+3+6+6=22

subject: Mathematics
q: The largest value of $n$, for which $40^{n}$ divides $60!$, is
question: The largest value of $n$, for which $40^{n}$ divides $60!$, is
solution:
$40^n = 2^{3n} \times 5^n$. We need the highest power of $40$ dividing $60!$.
Power of $5$ in $60! = \lfloor 60/5 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/25 \rfloor = 12 + 2 = 14$.
Power of $2$ in $60! = \lfloor 60/2 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/4 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/8 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/16 \rfloor + \lfloor 60/32 \rfloor = 30 + 15 + 7 + 3 + 1 = 56$.
We need $n \leq 14$ and $3n \leq 56$ (i.e., $n \leq 18$).
The binding constraint is $n \leq 14$, so the largest value is $n = 14$.

=(3!)3×4!

q: The number of numbers between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> that can be formed with the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> (repetition of digits is not allowed) and are multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is
question: The number of numbers between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>000</mn></math> that can be formed with the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn></math> (repetition of digits is not allowed) and are multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math> is
solution:
When a number is divisible by 3, then sum of digits of a number is also divisible by 3.

Number can be formed from (0,1,2,3) or (0,2,3,4).

Number of 4 digits number

=3×3×2×1+3×3×2×1

=36.

AA, MM, TT, H, I, C, S, E

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of seven-digit numbers, that can be formed by using the digits $1, 2, 3, 5$ and $7$ such that each digit is used at least once, is :
question: The number of seven-digit numbers, that can be formed by using the digits $1, 2, 3, 5$ and $7$ such that each digit is used at least once, is :
solution:
A seven-digit number is to be formed using the $5$ digits $1, 2, 3, 5,$ and $7$. Since each digit must be used at least once, the remaining $2$ places can be filled by either repeating one digit two more times, or repeating two digits one more time each.

This gives rise to two cases for the frequencies of the digits:

Case 1: One digit appears $3$ times and the other four digits appear $1$ time each.
The number of ways to choose the digit that appears $3$ times is $^{5}C_{1}$.
The number of arrangements of these $7$ digits is $\dfrac{7!}{3!}$.
Number of numbers formed in this case = $^{5}C_{1} \times \dfrac{7!}{3!} = 5 \times \dfrac{5040}{6} = 4200$.

Case 2: Two digits appear $2$ times each and the other three digits appear $1$ time each.
The number of ways to choose the two digits that appear $2$ times is $^{5}C_{2}$.
The number of arrangements of these $7$ digits is $\dfrac{7!}{2!2!}$.
Number of numbers formed in this case = $^{5}C_{2} \times \dfrac{7!}{2!2!} = 10 \times \dfrac{5040}{4} = 12600$.

Total number of seven-digit numbers = $4200 + 12600 = 16800$.

Answer: $16800$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the number of 5-element subsets of the set $A=\left\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{20}\right\}$ of 20 distinct elements is $k$ times the number of 5-element subsets containing $a_4$, then $k$ is
question: If the number of 5-element subsets of the set $A=\left\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{20}\right\}$ of 20 distinct elements is $k$ times the number of 5-element subsets containing $a_4$, then $k$ is
solution:
Set $A=\left\{a_1, a_2, \ldots . ., a_{20}\right\}$ has 20 distinct elements.
We have to select 5-element subset.
$\therefore$ Number of 5-element subsets $={ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_5$
According to question
$$
\begin{aligned}
& { }^{20} \mathrm{C}_5=\left({ }^{19} \mathrm{C}_4 \cdot k\right. \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{20 !}{5 ! 15 !}=k \cdot\left(\frac{19 !}{4 ! 15 !}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{20}{5}=k \Rightarrow k=4
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of arrangements that can be formed from the letters $a, b, c, d, e, f$ taken 3 at a time without repetition and each arrangement containing at least one vowel, is
question: The number of arrangements that can be formed from the letters $a, b, c, d, e, f$ taken 3 at a time without repetition and each arrangement containing at least one vowel, is
solution:
There are 2 vowels and 4 consonants in the letters $a, b, c, d, e, f$.
If we select one vowel, then number of arrangements
$$
={ }^2 C_1 \times{ }^4 C_2 \times 3 !=2 \times \frac{4 \times 3}{2} \times 3 \times 2=72
$$
If we select two vowels, then number of arrangements
$$
={ }^2 C_2 \times{ }^4 C_1 \times 3 !=1 \times 4 \times 6=24
$$
Hence, total number of arrangements
$$
=72+24=96
$$

q: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit odd numbers, whose sum of digits is a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math>, is _____.
question: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit odd numbers, whose sum of digits is a multiple of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn></math>, is _____.
solution:
Let the three-digit odd number be xyz

Now z=1/3/5/7/9

x+y+z=7/14/21  [for sum of digit to be multiple of 7]

x+y =6/4/2/13/11/9/7/5/20/18/16/14/12

When x+y=6⇒1,5,2,4,3,3,4,2,5,1,6,0

Total possibilities=6

When x+y=4⇒1,3,2,2,3,1,4,0

Total possibilities=4

When x+y=2⇒1,1,2,0

Total possibilities=2

When x+y=13⇒4,9,5,8,6,7,7,6,8,5,9,4

Total possibilities=6

When x+y=11⇒2,9,3,8,4,7,5,6,6,5,6,5,7,4,8,3,9,2

Total possibilities=8

When x+y=9⇒1,8,2,7,3,8,4,5,5,4,….8,1,9,0

Total possibilities=9

When x+y=7⇒1,8,2,5,3,4,….8,1,7,0

Total possibilities=7

When x+y=5⇒1,4,2,3,3,2,4,1,5,0

Total possibilities=5

When x+y=20  No possibilities

When x+y=18⇒9,9

Total possibilities=1

When x+y=16⇒7,9,8,8,9,7

Total possibilities=3

When x+y=14⇒5,9,6,8,7,7,8,6,9,5

Total possibilities=5

When x+y=12⇒3,9,4,8,5,7,6,6….9,3

Total possibilities=7

Hence, the total number of three digit odd numbers whose sum of digit is divisible by 7 is 63

q: If ${ }^n C_r$ denotes the number of combination of $n$ things taken $r$ at a time, then the expression ${ }^n C_{r+1}+{ }^n C_{r-1}+2 x^n C_r$ equals
question: If ${ }^n C_r$ denotes the number of combination of $n$ things taken $r$ at a time, then the expression ${ }^n C_{r+1}+{ }^n C_{r-1}+2 x^n C_r$ equals
solution:
After t; velocity $=\mathrm{f} \times \mathrm{t}$
$V_{B A}=\vec{f} t+(-\vec{u})=\sqrt{f^2 t^2+u^2-2 f u t \cos \alpha}$
For max. and min.

Therefore, total no. of values of $\mathrm{r}=33$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A=\{(a,b,c): a,b,c \text{ are non-negative integers and } a+b+2c=22\}$. Then $n(A)$ is equal to:
question: Let $A=\{(a,b,c): a,b,c \text{ are non-negative integers and } a+b+2c=22\}$. Then $n(A)$ is equal to:
solution:
The given equation is $a + b + 2c = 22$, where $a, b, c$ are non-negative integers.

This can be rewritten as $a + b = 22 - 2c$.

Since $a \ge 0$ and $b \ge 0$, we must have $22 - 2c \ge 0$, which gives $c \le 11$.

Since $c$ is a non-negative integer, the possible values for $c$ are $0, 1, 2, \dots, 11$.

For a fixed value of $c$, the number of non-negative integer solutions to the equation $a + b = 22 - 2c$ is given by $(22 - 2c) + 1 = 23 - 2c$.

The total number of solutions $n(A)$ is the sum of the number of solutions for each possible value of $c$:

$n(A) = \sum_{c=0}^{11} (23 - 2c)$

$n(A) = 23 + 21 + 19 + \dots + 1$

This is an arithmetic progression with $12$ terms, first term $1$, and last term $23$.

$n(A) = \dfrac{12}{2} (1 + 23) = 6 \times 24 = 144$

Answer: $144$

subject: Mathematics
q: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit numbers, whose greatest common divisor with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, is ______.
question: The total number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-digit numbers, whose greatest common divisor with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, is ______.
solution:
Prime factorisation of 36=2×2×3×3

So, for number having G.C.D with 36 to be 2, the number should be odd multiple of 2 and does not have factor 3 and 9

⇒Odd multiple of 2 are

102,106,110,114…….998  225 numbers

⇒No. of multiples of 3 are

102,114,126…….990 75 numbers

Which also include multiple of 9

Hence, Required =225-75=150

subject: Mathematics
q: From 6 different novels and 3 different dictionaries, 4 novels and 1 dictionary are to be selected and arranged in a row on the shelf so that the dictionary is always in the middle. Then the number of such arrangements is
question: From 6 different novels and 3 different dictionaries, 4 novels and 1 dictionary are to be selected and arranged in a row on the shelf so that the dictionary is always in the middle. Then the number of such arrangements is
solution:
4 novels can be selected from 6 novels in ${ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4$ ways. 1 dictionary can be selected from 3 dictionaries in ${ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1$ ways. As the dictionary selected is fixed in the middle, the remaining 4 novels can be arranged in 4 ! ways.
$\therefore$ The required number of ways of arrangement $={ }^6 \mathrm{C}_4 \times{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \times 4 !=1080$

question: The number of different 5 digit numbers greater than 50000 that can be formed using the digits 0 , $1,2,3,4,5,6,7$, such that the sum of their first and last digits should not be more than 8 , is
solution:
$\begin{array}{lll}
\text { Case I } & 5 \ldots \ldots & 0 \\
\text { Case II } & 5 \ldots \ldots & \\
& 5 & 1 \\
& 5 & 3 \\
& 6 & 0 \\
& 6 & 1 \\
& 6 & 2 \\
& 7 & 0 \\
& 7 & 1
\end{array}$

$9 \times(8 \times 8 \times 8)=4608$ but 50000 is not included, so total numbers $4608-1=4607$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of permutations, of the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> without repetition, which neither contain the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>153</mn></math> nor the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2467</mn></math>, is _______ .
question: The number of permutations, of the digits <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>7</mn></math> without repetition, which neither contain the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>153</mn></math> nor the string <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2467</mn></math>, is _______ .
solution:
Given,

The number 1,2,3,....,7

Now total numbers of 7 digit number will be 7! without repetition,

Now total numbers which contain string 154 will be 5!

Total numbers which contain string 2367 will be 4!

And number which contain both string 2367 & 154 will be 2

So, total number which does not contain string 154 or 2367 will be 7!-5!+4!-2

=5040-120+24-2

=5040-142=4898

Hence this is the required answer.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of words, with or without meaning, that can be formed by taking 4 letters at a time from the letters of the word 'SYLLABUS' such that two letters are distinct and two letters are alike, is
question: The number of words, with or without meaning, that can be formed by taking 4 letters at a time from the letters of the word 'SYLLABUS' such that two letters are distinct and two letters are alike, is
solution:
From the given word, S-2,L-2,A,B,Y,U.

Number of alike letter pairs =2

Number of ways to select one pair=C12

Number of distinct letters=5

Number of ways to select two distinct letters=C25

Number of ways to arrange four letters if two are alike=4!2!

Required number of ways =C12C25·4!2!=2·10·242=240

question: A test consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> multiple choice questions, each having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> alternative answers of which only one is correct. The number of ways, in which a candidate answers all six questions such that exactly four of the answers are correct, is ___________
solution:
No. of ways of giving wrong answer =3

So, required no. of ways =C46(1)4×(3)2
=15(9)=135

q: All the arrangements, with or without meaning, of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> are written excluding any word that has two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> appearing together. The arrangements are listed serially in the alphabetic order as in the English dictionary. Then the serial number of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> in this list is _____ .
question: All the arrangements, with or without meaning, of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> are written excluding any word that has two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> appearing together. The arrangements are listed serially in the alphabetic order as in the English dictionary. Then the serial number of the word <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>FARMER</mi></math> in this list is _____ .
solution:
Number of such words without both R together

= total possible words - total possible words with both R together

Number of such words start with A=5!2!-4!=36

Number of such words start with E=5!2!-4!=36

Number of such words start with FAE=3!2!-2=1

Number of such words start with FAM=3!2!-2=1

Number of such words start with FARE=2!=2

Number of such words start with FARMER=1

Total =77

q: The number of words, with or without meaning, that can be formed using all the letters of the word ASSASSINATION so that the vowels occur together, is _____ .
question: The number of words, with or without meaning, that can be formed using all the letters of the word ASSASSINATION so that the vowels occur together, is _____ .
solution:
In the word ASSASSINATION, we will treat all the vowels that is AAAIIO  as one letter only.

Now, we have AAAIIOSSSSNNT

This has 8=(7 + 1) letters of which S occurs 4 times, and N occur 2 times,

Thus, the number of ways of  arranging these letters =8!4!2!=840.

Now, 6 vowels in which A occurs 3 times, and I occur two times, they can be arranged in 6!3!2!=60  ways. 

 Therefore, required number of ways= (840 × 60) = 50400.
